1: Imru le-acheikhem Ammi -- 'Say to your brothers, My people.' Ch 2 opens with the reversal of the children's names: Lo-ammi becomes Ammi, Lo-ruhamah becomes Ruhamah. Ve-la-achotekhem Ruhamah -- 'and to your sisters, My beloved/pitied.' This is the answer to Hosea 1:6, 9: the 'not loved' is now addressed as 'loved.' The community's task is to speak these reversed names -- to declare the covenant restoration before it has fully arrived. The NT appropriation (1 Peter 2:9-10) addresses the church with the Hosea restoration language: 'Once you were not a people, but now you are God's people; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy.'
2: Rivu ve-imeikhem rivu -- 'Contend with your mother, contend.' The legal language of riv (H7378, to contend/bring a case/plead) opens a covenant lawsuit. The legal setting is a formal accusation: YHWH is the aggrieved party, Israel (the mother) is the defendant, and her children (the individual Israelites) are the witnesses and litigants. Ki hi lo-ishti ve-anokhi lo-ishah -- 'for she is not my wife, and I am not her husband.' The formal pronouncement of divorce in ancient Near Eastern law. The covenant marriage is dissolved by formal declaration -- the legal obverse of the covenant formula. Ve-tasir zenutehah mi-paneha -- 'let her remove her prostitution from her face' -- the call to repentance that underlies the lawsuit: the legal action is not final punishment but a call to amendment.
3: Pen-achasot ha-imah -- 'or else I will strip her naked.' The warning of covenant punishment: Ve-hitzagtiha ke-yom hullaledet -- 'and expose her as on the day she was born.' Birth-nakedness -- total vulnerability and shame. Ve-samtia ka-midbar -- 'and make her like a wilderness.' The covenant curse of land desolation (Leviticus 26:33; Deuteronomy 28:23-24) is framed as the reversal of the Exodus gift: YHWH brought Israel from wilderness to fruitful land; covenant violation returns the land to wilderness. Ve-hamittia ba-tzimmaon -- 'and parched with thirst.' The images of aridity (wilderness, parched) contrast with the covenant promises of water, rain, and fruitful land (Hosea 2:21-22).
4: Ve-et-baneha lo arachem -- 'I will show no compassion to her children.' The children of the unfaithful mother (Israel) share in the judgment -- not because they are individually guilty, but because covenant unfaithfulness corrupts the entire household. Ki benei zenutim hemmah -- 'for they are children of unfaithfulness.' Zenutim (prostitutions/unfaithfulness) -- not merely the children of a prostitute but the offspring of a spiritually adulterous society. The punishment of children for parental sin raises the theodicy question that Ezekiel 18 addresses directly -- Hosea leaves the tension standing rather than resolving it, reflecting the corporate solidarity of the covenant.
5: Ki zantah immam -- 'For their mother has been unfaithful.' The mother's statement is quoted: Elekhah acharei meahavai -- 'I will go after my lovers.' Meatim (my lovers, H157) -- the Baals and the fertility cult partners Israel pursued. The shocking claim: Notenii lachmi u-meimi tzamri u-fishti shimni u-shiquvai -- 'who give me my bread and my water, my wool and my flax, my oil and my drink.' Israel has attributed the covenant gifts (grain, wine, oil -- the standard triad of Deuteronomy 7:13) to the Baals rather than to YHWH. The diagnostic error is one of mis-attribution: the gifts are real but the giver is misidentified. This error is the root of idolatry as Hosea understands it.
6: Lachen hineni sach et-darkech ba-sirim -- 'Therefore I will block her way with thorns.' The divine response to the pursuit of false lovers: YHWH blocks the paths with thorns (sirim, H5518) and builds a wall (gadar, H1447) so she cannot reach her destinations. The image is not primarily punitive but remedial -- the blocking is designed to redirect, not destroy. The thorns and wall prevent the prostitution from succeeding so that she might return to the first husband. This is the coercive mercy of the covenant: YHWH's frustration of Israel's idolatry as a means of bringing her back.
7: Ve-ridefeah et-meahaveyha ve-lo tasiag otam -- 'She will chase after her lovers but not catch them.' The pursuit ends in failure: the Baals cannot be found when Israel seeks them. Ve-biqqeshtam ve-lo timtza -- 'she will seek them but not find them.' The failure of the false lovers is YHWH's doing through the wall and thorns of Hosea 2:6. Ve-amrah elkhah ve-ashuva el-ishi ha-rishon -- 'then she will say, I will go and return to my first husband.' Ishi ha-rishon (my first husband) -- YHWH as the original marriage partner, the covenant relationship prior to the Baals. Ki tov li az mi-attah -- 'for it was better for me then than now.' The comparative judgment: the covenant life is better than the idolatrous life, even from the perspective of material provision. The return is motivated by experiential memory, not merely theological instruction.
8: Ve-hi lo yadah ki anokhi natatti lah ha-dagan ve-ha-tirosh ve-ha-yitzhar -- 'She did not know that it was I who gave her the grain, the wine, and the oil.' The tragedy of ignorance: Lo yadah (she did not know) -- yada (H3045, to know) is the key verb of Hosea's theology. The 'knowledge of God' (daath Elohim) is not merely intellectual but covenantal -- to know YHWH is to recognize him as the source of all covenant gifts. Israel's idolatry is fundamentally an epistemological failure: she received good gifts but attributed them to the wrong giver. Ve-chasef va-zahav hirbu lahem asher asu la-Baal -- 'and who lavished on her silver and gold, which they used for Baal.' Even the wealth Israel used to craft idols was YHWH's gift. The idols were funded by the God they displaced.
9: Lachen ashuv ve-laqachti degani be-ito ve-tiroshi be-moadо -- 'Therefore I will take back my grain when it ripens and my wine when it is ready.' YHWH's reclamation of the covenant gifts he gave: the possessive pronoun is emphatic -- degani (my grain), tiroshi (my wine) -- the gifts remain YHWH's even when given to Israel. Taking them back is the act of a husband removing the household provision from an unfaithful wife. Ve-hitzalti tzamri ve-fishti le-khassot et-ervatah -- 'I will reclaim my wool and my flax, which were used to cover her nakedness.' The exposure of nakedness (Hosea 2:3) is the punishment; the removal of the covering garments is the instrument. The covenant covered Israel's shame; covenant violation means the covering is removed.
10: Ve-attah agaleh et-navlutatah le-einei meahaveyha -- 'Now I will expose her shameful nakedness before her lovers.' Navlut (H5040, lewdness/shameful nakedness) -- public exposure as the covenant curse for unfaithfulness (cf. Ezekiel 16:37-39; Nahum 3:5). The exposure before the lovers is the ultimate shame: those she sought to please will witness her punishment. Ve-ish lo-yatzzilenah mi-yyadi -- 'and no one will rescue her from my hand.' The powerlessness of the Baals: they cannot protect the one who worshipped them when YHWH acts in judgment. The false lovers provide nothing at the moment of crisis -- the exact opposite of YHWH's covenant protection.
11: Ve-hishbatti kol-mesosah chagah chadshah ve-shabbatah -- 'I will put an end to all her celebrations -- her festivals, her new moon observances, her sabbaths.' The cessation of the cultic calendar is the withdrawal of covenant worship-structure. Chag (H2282, pilgrimage festival), chodesh (H2320, new moon), Shabbat (H7676, Sabbath) -- the three principal liturgical rhythms of Israel's covenant life. Their suspension is not mere punishment but the logical consequence of covenant dissolution: if the covenant is annulled, the covenant-ordered time cannot continue. The withdrawal of liturgical time mirrors the exile's disruption of sacred geography.
12: Ve-hacharvoti gafnah ve-teenatah -- 'I will ruin her vines and her fig trees.' The specific crops named are the covenant gifts Israel attributed to the Baals (Hosea 2:5). Asher amrah etnan hemah li asher natenu li meahavai -- 'which she said were the gifts her lovers gave her.' The ruin makes visible the falseness of her attribution: the Baals cannot sustain what YHWH withdraws. Ve-samtim le-yaar -- 'I will make them a forest.' Cultivated land reverting to wilderness -- the un-gardening of the covenant land. Ve-akhalatan chayat ha-sadeh -- 'and wild animals will devour them.' The wild animals represent the undoing of the agricultural order that covenant life maintains.
13: Ve-faqadti aleha et-yemei ha-Baalim -- 'I will punish her for the days she burned incense to the Baals.' Faqad (H6485, to visit/appoint/punish) -- divine attention for judgment. Asher tiqter lahem va-tadas nizmatah ve-cheliyatah va-telekh acharei meahaveyha -- 'when she adorned herself with rings and jewelry and went after her lovers.' The adorning is the preparation for cult prostitution -- dressing for the lover, not the husband. Ve-oti shakhachah -- 'and forgot me.' Shakach (H7911, to forget) -- the opposite of zachor (to remember/be faithful). Forgetting YHWH is the active form of apostasy in Hosea: not merely ignorance but willful erasure of the covenant relationship. The verb is used eight times in Hosea.
14: Lachen hineh anokhi mefateyah -- 'Therefore, behold, I will allure her.' The great reversal: after all the judgment language (Hosea 2:3-13), the divine strategy pivots to wooing. Mefateyah (H6601, to allure/entice/persuade) -- the same verb used in Exodus 22:16 for seduction. YHWH woos his unfaithful wife back not by force but by persuasion. Ve-holakhtiha ha-midbar -- 'and bring her into the wilderness.' The wilderness is not only the place of judgment (Hosea 2:3) but the place of covenant origin -- the Sinai wilderness where YHWH first spoke tenderly to Israel. The new exodus leads through a new wilderness encounter. Ve-dibarti al-libah -- 'and speak tenderly to her.' Al-lev (to the heart) -- the language of intimate reassurance (cf. Genesis 50:21; Ruth 2:13; Isaiah 40:2). YHWH speaks to the heart, not merely to the legal standing, of his estranged wife.
15: Ve-natatti lah et-keramehah mi-sham -- 'There I will give back her vineyards.' The restoration mirrors and reverses the judgment: the vineyards that were ruined (Hosea 2:12) are given back. Ve-et-emeq Akhor le-petach tiqvah -- 'and make the Valley of Achor a door of hope.' Akhor (H5911, trouble/distress) -- the valley where Achan was executed for covenant violation (Joshua 7:24-26). The place of covenant failure and punishment becomes the threshold of hope. The most troubled geography of Israel's past becomes the entrance to the restored future. Ve-antetah sham ke-yemei neureiha -- 'she will respond there as in the days of her youth.' Anah (H6030, to respond/answer) -- Israel's response to YHWH restored to the freshness of the Exodus. The honeymoon of Sinai recreated.
16: Ve-hayah ba-yom ha-hu neum-YHWH -- 'In that day, declares the LORD.' The oracular formula marks the eschatological turning point. Tiqrei Ishi -- 'you will call me My Husband.' Ishi (H376+suffix, my man/husband) -- the term of intimate spousal address. Ve-lo tiqrei-li od Baali -- 'and you will no longer call me My Master/Lord.' Baali (H1168+suffix, my lord/Baal) -- the identical form used for the deity Baal and for a husband's authority title. The pun is deliberate: Israel will stop addressing YHWH with the title that sounds like the Baals' name. The new relationship will have a new relational vocabulary -- one of intimacy (ishi, husband) rather than hierarchy (baali, lord). The covenant is renewed on the terms of love rather than mere legal obligation.
17: Va-hasiroti et-shemot ha-Baalim mi-piha -- 'For I will remove the names of the Baals from her lips.' The Baal names -- which were also spoken as divine titles in worship -- will be purged from Israel's language. The mouth that called on Baal will speak only of YHWH. Ve-lo-yizzakheru od bi-shemam -- 'and they will be mentioned no more.' The complete erasure of the Baals from covenant vocabulary. In Hosea's theology, naming and knowing are inseparable: to stop naming the Baals is to stop relating to them as if they existed. The purification of language is the prerequisite for the purification of worship.
18: Ve-karati lahem berit ba-yom ha-hu -- 'In that day I will make a covenant for them.' The eschatological covenant encompasses the entire natural order. Im-chayat ha-sadeh ve-im-of ha-shamayim ve-remes ha-adamah -- 'with the wild animals, the birds of the sky, and the creatures of the ground.' The covenant with creation reverses the covenant curse of Hosea 2:12 (wild animals devouring the land). Ve-qeshet ve-cherev u-milchamah eshbor min-ha-aretz -- 'and I will abolish the bow, the sword, and warfare from the land.' The bow that was broken in judgment (Hosea 1:5) is now broken in peace: the same act has a completely different meaning in the restoration. Ve-hishkavtim la-vetach -- 'and I will make them lie down in safety.' The covenant peace: Israel resting secure in the land, as a flock under its shepherd.
19: Ve-erastikh li le-olam -- 'I will betroth you to me forever.' The triple betrothal formula (Hosea 2:19-20) is the pinnacle of Hosea's restoration promise. Aras (H781, to betroth) -- the formal engagement that precedes marriage; YHWH proposes a new beginning to the covenant marriage, starting from engagement rather than from the existing marital state. Le-olam (H5769, forever/into the age) -- the betrothal will not be temporary or conditional in the way the Sinai covenant was. Ve-erastikh li be-tzedeq u-ve-mishpat u-ve-chesed u-ve-rachamim -- 'I will betroth you in righteousness and justice, in covenant loyalty and compassion.' Four covenant attributes are the dowry of the new marriage: tzedeq (righteousness), mishpat (justice), chesed (covenant loyalty), rachamim (compassion/mercy). These are the character-qualities that the Sinai covenant's institutional forms were meant to express -- now given as the basis of relationship itself.
20: Ve-erastikh li be-emunah -- 'I will betroth you in faithfulness.' Emunah (H530, faithfulness/reliability/steadfastness) -- the quality of being dependable over time; the same root as amen. The fifth attribute of the new betrothal covenant. Ve-yadaat et-YHWH -- 'and you will know the LORD.' The climactic result of the five-fold betrothal is knowledge of YHWH -- daath YHWH. This is Hosea's central term: the covenant intimacy that Israel abandoned (Hosea 4:1, 6) is the promised outcome of the restoration. The knowledge of God is not merely intellectual information but the experiential intimacy of a restored marriage. Paul's statement 'I know him whom I have believed' (2 Timothy 1:12) and John's 'this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God' (John 17:3) stand in Hosea's tradition.
21: Ve-hayah ba-yom ha-hu aeneh neum-YHWH -- 'In that day I will respond, declares the LORD.' Anah (H6030, to respond/answer) -- YHWH initiates the chain of responses that will restore the broken order. Aeneh et-ha-shamayim ve-hem yaanou et-ha-aretz -- 'I will respond to the heavens, and they will respond to the earth.' A cascade of responses: YHWH speaks to the heavens, heavens respond to earth, earth responds to crops. The creation order, broken by the covenant curse (Hosea 4:3: the land mourns), is restored by YHWH's covenantal faithfulness flowing through the creation hierarchy. The agricultural provision that Israel attributed to the Baals (Hosea 2:5) is restored as a gift transparently from YHWH through the created order.
22: Ve-ha-aretz taaneh et-ha-dagan ve-et-ha-tirosh ve-et-ha-yitzhar -- 'and the earth will respond to the grain, the wine, and the oil.' The triad of covenant agricultural gifts (dagan, tirosh, yitzhar) that Israel attributed to Baal (Hosea 2:5) now flow from YHWH through the responsive creation order. Ve-hem yaanou et-Yizreel -- 'and they will respond to Jezreel.' The name Jezreel ('God sows') becomes the climactic term: what began as the name of judgment (Hosea 1:4-5) ends as the name of agricultural restoration. God sows the seed; the chain of cosmic response produces the harvest; Jezreel is both the people who were scattered and re-sown and the land that produces the grain. The judgment name becomes the restoration name.
23: Ve-zara'tiha li ba-aretz -- 'I will plant her for myself in the land.' Zara (H2232, to sow/plant) -- the literal fulfillment of Jezreel ('God sows'). YHWH personally sows Israel back into the covenant land after the exile-scattering. Ve-richamti et-Lo-Ruhamah -- 'and I will show mercy to the one called Lo-ruhamah.' The daughter whose name denied compassion is now given compassion -- the passive reversal of the Lo-ruhamah sentence. Ve-amarti le-Lo-Ammi ammi atah -- 'and I will say to Lo-ammi, You are my people.' The greatest reversal: the dissolution of the covenant formula in 1:9 is undone. 'You are my people' reinstates the broken covenant. Ve-hu yomar Elohai -- 'and he will say, My God.' The bilateral response: YHWH says 'my people'; Israel says 'my God.' Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:25 for Gentile inclusion: the covenant restoration language applies to all who were 'not my people' and are now included in Christ.
